J.P. THOMAS DAVIS.

By By T. W. RorLestoN, M.A.
= When Thomas Davis was carried to his grave in Mount Jerome his
rry remains were followed by a mourning throng of all that was noblest
ynt, | and worthiest in the Irish world of his time. Yet among all who
! mourned for that young life so tragically cut short—
SEE ‘““ Not dying as he yearmed to die,
Keened by his country’s viactor-cry,
> But struck by swift and stern disease,”
T there were few ‘who ocould have foreseen that seveuty years later such 3

a gathering as this could assemble in London, and at so anxious a
* time, to do honour to his memory and to call to mind the teaohmg
~ and the example which he left to his countrymen. IFor what tanglble
achievement did he leave behind him? A few poems, two or three
i Essays on history or economics, a mass of anonymous articles in the o
¢ Nation ’’ newspaper. The great gift that Davis made to Ireland— | =
i none of theso—was thaf of his own wonderful personality. It was
the whole bent and temper of his mind—his way of looking at things—
V. the manner and style of his thought about Ireland, that brought a new
| foroe into Irish history. Amnd the revelation of all this, it must never
. be forgotten, we owe to his life-long and loyal friend, Charles Gavan o
@ Duffy. I know nothing finer in its way than the pure unclouded affec- |
_- “;-‘f_l':?ion! and unity of high purpose which united these two great Irishmen B
' —omne o Protestant, one a Oatholic, or than the living portrait which |
f"bhe survivor has left us of the character and spirit of hms friend. To
ha.v& given us that portrait—to have set it there as an ‘example and
lm inspiration to all Irishmen who, in any sphere, wish to do a stroke
7 S of work for their native land—was, in my opinion, the greatest of all
b 'be great services which Sir Charles Duffy rendered to Ireland.
The public career of which Sir Charles Duffy has given us this
' :r 0 cord lasted only throe years. I dato it from the foundation of the
20 3 “W&ﬁwn " newspaper, of which the first number appeared on October

3 " _ L‘: z "’
at -";'.

i

50 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

15th, 1842. Before this period Davis had been known first as a shy .'

and dreamy schoolboy with mo taste for the ordinary work of his olag 2
and considered, in fact, something of a dunce. His family, on he
father’s side, ‘were of Welsh origin, but had been settled for some tlme o
in Buckinghamshire. His mother was one of the Atkinses of Firville : w;

Co. Cork, a family of Cromwellian origin though they had inter. B

married at one time with the O’Sullivans of Beare, and from that ™

high source the best Irish blood ran in the veins of Davis. Tha' 0T

family on both sides among whom Davis grew up were of the strictest:  r

Unionist principles, and it is not easy to put one’s finger on the pm-. 4

cise moment when he separated from them politically, nor on the cause E

of what they mo doubt lamented as his” defection. The change, at any’ o

T rate, was complete when he was an undergraduate in Trinity College b
—and here he certainly never could have been taken for a dunce. He : v
quickly won. a position of influence among his fellow students. He g
read incessantly, particularly in the fields of History, Iconomaics, and €
Ethics. Afterwards, when called to the Bar, he practised a little
it the Revision Courts, but with no great energy, and dabbled in political .‘
T journalism with the only result of calling down upon himself a goody
A deal of contemptuous banter from the hack-politicians who surrounded
O’Connell, and who, from the beginning to the end, showed themselves! &
wholly incapable of comprehending the spirit and the ideals whwh F'
; T Thomas Davis brought into Irish political life. E
A All this time, while he may have appeared to be a mere dilettante! B -
trifling on the fringe of serious life, he wasn mahty—-though pe:rhaps :
unconsciously—preparing himself for a great mission. His studies
were both wide- and deep—he read with the closest attention, as the
marginal motes in many of his books testify—and he read constantly
with an eye to Irish problems in the present and an the past. Ult-ml-
. ately, when he came to turn his gifts and his acquirements to prac-
: f;‘ ,4. »35 tical objects, he brought into play a fertility of thought, an amphtude
: of kmowledge, a faculty for drawing counsels, 1Jlrusrl:.mtmns : wm'nnnga ,
ﬁuggesvtmm, from the historical and intellectual life of the European
SE people-s : which were something altogether new in Inish politics an
rish journslism. 0t may be said, I think, that for the first time 11

- % @“ufb Treland on the world-stage in the eyes of the Irish people. B
s emus'bomed his countrymen to rbhink of their owmn pl*obl@nm ) a..s.pm

el L

.f—df {‘"‘ rz ons <1 shmggles m a 'btroad humaan, unt@]hgant way as & h;rt’h

. A
. ¥ %

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

European history—not contemptuously on the one hand, as the
scufflings of savages in a remote corner of the West, nor on the other
hand in the false light of that hysterical, ranting kind of patriotism
which was a perfect disease in the Ireland of the time of Davis, and
was not a little encouraged by O’Connell. Davis was a scholar, but
his work was mnot done 1 the arena of scholarship, but among the
{  people; and in the sphere of history this work was, at the time, unique
¢ in its value and character. Tt is difficult for us in these days to
realise the state of things which prevailed in Davis’s time in regard
to historical kmowledge of and in Ireland. The people in gemeral—
even those who passed for educated—Nationalist and Unionist alike—
could not, says Duffy, have told whether the Battle of Benburb came
¢ before or after the battle of Clontarf, or whether Owen Roe O’Neill
¢ was a maked savage armed with a stake or one of the most accom-
plished soldiers in Europe. Davis was the first who, without false
d,  exaggeration, spread among the people, by essay, by song, and nob
»  least by the work executed by other hands, but suggested and inspired
J by him a just pride in the history of their race and a true knowledge
i of it. And not of the Celtic race alone. Davis knew well of what a
i number of ancient and admirable stocks the modern JIrish nation 1s
s, composed, and no son of any of them, from the Phenician to the
,,‘ Cromwellian, was a foreigner to Davis, so long as he did not insist on
regarding himself as an alien and a stranger in Ireland.  Still, he
e recognised that the root-stock of the population was Celtic, and he
s was the first Irish public man, I think, to turn the minds of the
5. people with special reverence towards their Celtic ancestry and the
e Celtic language and literature. Wolfe Tone never did that, Emmet
~ did not do it, O’Connell did not do it. Moore, indeed, attempted some-
- thing of the kind, but ho threw over Celtic Ireland a certain false air
~ of eighteenth century eclegance and sentiment far removed from the b
realities. Davigs set all our thinking about Celtic Treland in a new - gl
~ key—the key of veracity, of sympathy and of wespect, out of which
| j“'f‘.a'lfmﬁ a gonuine understanding can come.
]  In politics Davis was, of course, a Repealer, a member of 0’Con- TR
;' nell’s Asgociation, hut it does mot appear that he had time in his
ﬁhOI"b public life to think out this question to an end. Sir C. Duffy
| v‘ﬁﬁus us that at ono time, in the year bofore his death, he was well

-*dISDOsed to a solution of the national question on fodoral lines. It 1s

e b —

52 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. &

true that it is impossible to turn over his pages without perceiving 1809,

the presence of a vehemently anti-English feeling. But at that time
what other feeling could any Irishman have who loved his country aald 1812,
who had any sense of her nafionality? The England of the present? |
day—not to speak of history which is still in the making—the England 1812,
which has been spending and lending millions to root the Iush

peasant in his mative soil, which has introduced full religious equa,hty,

which has established the Congested Districts Board, a source of in-

finite blessings to the population of the West, which has set up ﬂlﬁh 1813,
Department of Agriculture and Industries with 1ts large populargf-
representation, in response to a purely Irish demand, which has estab--
lished free local government all over Ireland, and, finally, which mEf_
spending thousands every year—I believe the amount is only hlmted
by the willingness of the people to take advantage of it—ifor enoourag— >

ing the learning of Gaelic in the National Schools which knows neither

1814.

e 14
garrisons nor ascendencies nor privilege of any shape or kind—an En-.g— L 1814

land which has done all these things would have seemed to Davis andf"‘"
the men of his time something almost incredible, and if it had oome-*,

into being in his time a nature like his, so frank, so candid, so gener- ?-4:“ (181

ous, would mndoubtedly have felt the aspect of the Irish question in {;g;.
relation to Great Britain altogether changed. Most certainly he would “-_ ,
never have ceased to be an Irish Nationalist—Nationalism with hlm: "' AR
rested on an altogether different basis from that of grievances. But i:{

I am convinced he would have felt that the possibility of free and E

e
T

182:

oordial co-operation with the people of Great Britain in one empire,

under one flag, in the work of civilisation and progress, had come p;; 189:

fairly into sight, and he would have devoted to this co-operation those | QL___ '

great qualities of heart and mind whose dawning promise were alli

tha‘o Fate permitted us to sce. »;;T_-: 189

THE IRISH BRIGADE.

| From the North to the South as a bird in its flight, ,_

From Cape Malin away to Carnsore, "';

They are flocking together and spoiling to fight
For the ravished and tortured in war.
The thud of their marching is shaking the earth
As they swing in close column arrayed
Whether Munster or Ulster’s the place of their birth,

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

R. Y. TYRRELL.

We regret to announce the death of the distinguished Irish soholar
Dr. Robert Yelverton Tyrrell, S.F.T.C.D., which took place at his
residence, Greystones, on Saturday,. 19th September. |

To all lovers of fine, fastidious scholarship the death of Dr, Tyrrell
must come with the shock of personal loss. Perfect felicity of diction,
a faultless sense of style, the light playful touch of the critic who is

'no pedant, a pure love of poetic form, and especially of the elegant

and ornate in poetry, made Tyrrell the wonderful ‘ classic’’ he was.
As a verse translator he had hardly a rival, and none can turn over
his many contributions to ‘‘ Kottabos ’’—that most brilliant of umni-
versity magazines—without marvelling at their variety, wit, and happy
charm. He possessed 1n an extraordinary degree that instinct for the
‘““mot juste’ which is the mark of the born translator, if not of the
born poet. This was his great gift. Omne sees it in his delightful
book on ‘‘ Latin Poetry ’’ (1895), as well as in his reprinted ‘‘ Dublin

Translations ’ (1882 and 1890) and  Fchoes from Kottabos’ (1906). .

One sees it on almost every page even of his school editions. In
everything that belongs to form, to style, to diction, in Greek and
Latin poetry, Tyrrell was incomparable.

Tyrrell was born at Ballingarry, Co. Tipperary, on Jan. 21st, 1844,
the youngest son of the Rev. Henry Tyrrell. FFrom a private school he
passed into Trinity College, Dublin, where he took nearly every honour
that came in his way. He won the Vice-Chancellor’s prize for Greek
verse with ‘ Hypatia,’' took his ‘‘ Studentship’ and Moderatorship
in bhoth classics with first gold medal, and ethics and logic with gold
medal, all in 1864 ; became Professor of Latin in 1871, Regius Professor
of Greek, 1880-98, Senior Tutor and Public Orator, 1898, Professor of
Ancient History 1900 to 1904, when he was co-opted as Senior Fellow
in the room of Dr. Traill, who had been appointed Provost, and was
also appointed Registrar.

As tutor and professor ho oxercised a ponetrating influence in a
way all his own. Ho scomed to take mo troublo in preparing his
lecbu-ms, but would come into the hall with an obviously uncut book,
upon which hoe was expected to lecture, and with which his lecture had

perhaps tho smallest possible connoxion. But his lectures were rovela-
I tions of realms of poetry and imagination undreamdt of by the fascin-
I ated audlefnoe Ho would wander from subject to subject, pour oub

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

56

verses by the score, illustrate them in his matchless way, light them
up with his scintillating wit, till his listeners caught the fire, and
went away stimulated as they never were by ordinary college lectures,
No teacher was ever less systematic; none succeeded so well in inspir-
ing his pupils with his love of classical poetry and fastidious sense of
style. It was the same wath his conversation.
Like most University tutors and professors, Tyrrell began his pub-
lished works with school-books, but choice specimens of their class.
His great work as Professor of Latin was his eloborate edition of
Cicero’s ‘‘ Correspondence,” of which the first two volumes appeared
in 1871, and the other five—in which his successor in the Latin Chair,
Prof. Purser, collaborated, were issued by degrees up to 1901. It is
a monumental work and worthy of both scholars. His most popular
books, and those which present to educated readers the crecm of his
'u-i-pe scholarship, are his ‘‘ Latin Poetry’’ (1895), originally delivered
ag the Turnbull Lectures at the John Hopkins University, Baltimore,
and the ‘‘ Bssays in Greek Literature’ (1909), which consists chiefly
of articles originally published .in the ‘‘ Quarterly ‘Revie-w,” to which
as well as the ‘‘Saturday Review’ and other periodicals, he was a
frequent contributor. Since 1881 he was a Commissioner of National
Education in Ireland, and on the formation of the British Academy he
was among the 50 original members.—*‘‘ Times.”’

. RARE SLIGO MAGAZINE.

I have a copy of what must be, I think, one of the rarest of Irish
provincial magazines, as I can find no reference to it in any. library
catalogue or work of reference whatever. It is entitled ‘‘ The Weekly
Selector: or, Sligo Miscellaneous Magazine,”” and a short account of
it may be of interest to your readers, who have enjoyed descriptions of
similar rare publications in your pages. It is a four page 4to, 9 x 73,
cut down, two columns to a page; the first number is dated Tuesdav,
February 4th, 1812. Price 8d. ‘‘Printed and Published by A. Bolton,
King’s Arms, Oastle St., where Orders, Essays, etc., for this Maga-
zine will bo thankfully rccewed The magazine on tho whole bears
& olose resemblance to the “Tit Bits’’ style of periodical of our own
day, as indeed its title would lead ome to expect, its contents ** prin-

;clpally drawn from 'works of the first rank in Literary Merit.”” Page
8 15 devoted to two specimens of *f Original Pootry,” not of a very

f‘; hngh order, one, ‘‘Ode to & Rock,” by O'Carroll, the other “.A.n
st

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 57.

Enigma, for the Sligo Selector,”” which would lead one to infer that

- Samuel Dillon, is a frequent contributor of verse and topographical '
artioles, and an editorial note says, ‘‘ Our sweet little Ballina minstrel {‘

~ ghall not be forgotten,”” and next no. contains some verse signed
«H.” from that town. In the 16th mo. a serial tale entitled ‘‘ The

Qastle of Savina; or the Irishman in Italy,” commences, and runs for

seven weeks, no indication being given as to its authorship or source. AE
~ From an editorial note we find that the first no. soon ran out of print, ' ’f’
 but in the 25th we learn that ‘‘its Subscribers are certainly numerous, I
- but the Editor begs leave to observe that unless the half yearly Pay-
- ment of 5s. be made, the Publication must be altogether discontinued.
~ That this hint was not taken is evidenced by the fact that p. 216 of
no. 27, for Tuesday, August 4th, 1812, concludes with this notice:

.. Publication of the Selector after this day . . . and assures his
; Htaera.ry friends that -a portion of the Sligo Journal’”—of which he
B was also the proprietor and printer—‘‘shall be set apart for the |

P 4 IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.
. The centenary of Thomas Davis was celebrated by a dinner at
i A.Iiduerton’ s Hotel, Fleet Sfbreeﬁ on Wednesday, 14th October, to which S f
.: - a distinguished company, numbering over a hundred, sat down. Mrs.

Bophw Bryant, D.Sc., occupied the chair, and amongst- others present S
- were Judge Higgins (Victoria), Sir Vincent Evans, 'W. B. Yeats, A.P. Al

Graves Miss Geraldine Gavan Duffy, Ernest Rhys, Grace Rhys, ,

EDITOR’S GOSSLP. ir
The magazines for October are much occupied with the subject of
the preat war, to the exclusion of almost every other subject. To the :
““ Contemporary Review ’’ those two great Irish authorities on foreign
affairs, Dr. E. J. Dillon and Mrs. Emily Crawford, contribute m‘tic]es,t;
the first telling *“ Why Russia Went to War,” and the other giving
her ‘‘ Personal Recollecltions of 1870,”” when she endured the horrors
of the Commune. In ‘‘Blackwood” Colin Campbell recalls his ex-
periences ‘‘ With the Irish Ambulance in France, 1870-71,”” and Mr.
T. W. Rolleston deals with ** Literature and Politics in Modern Ger-
many ’’ 1n the Hibbert Journal.”
The ‘¢ British Review,” despite its name, devotes more space to
Irish affairs this month than any of the others. Katherine Tynan
contributes the inevitable poem “In War Time’’ ; E. C. K. an article
on ‘‘Ireland and Ifficiency,” whilst A. R. has a very interesting
account of ‘‘ Things as Seen by an Irish Parish Priest.” I'rom it we T
learn that ‘‘ a well-known Irishman of letters’’ is preparing a work on *
Archbishop Croke and his times, which is bound to be full of interest.
The article ‘‘ Tipperary,”” by Edward Thomas, in the ¢ English,” 1
inspired by the popular marching song, deals exclusively with English 5
sights and scenes, and not, as might be expected, with the Irish
county. In the ‘“ Army Review’ General Barter, a Cork man, dis- 1
ousses ‘‘ Raids in War,”” and Major Pope Hennessy, bearer of a name
once well known in Irish politics, contributes ‘“ A Note on Reference
Libraries.”’ o
I find the current part of “ Book Auction Records’’ issued by
Frank Karslake, of Hampstead, as interesting and useful as ever.
It contains the results of the sale of the fourth portion of the famous
Huth Library at Sotheby’s in July, when the following items were
disposed of : Taithful History of the Northern Affairs of Ireland,
4to., 1690, £11 6s.; Report by Commissioners on Forfeited Estates,
4to., £8; Some Thoughts Humbly offerd towards an Union, 4to., 1708,
£5 12s. 6d.; Thoughts on the Bill Prohibiting Exportation of Woollen

[ soo that Mr. H. A. Hinkson has been appointed R.M. for Castle-
bar. Mr. Hinkson, who is the husband of Katherine Tynan, has
already some score of movels to his name, and has been by turns a
classical tutor at Clongowes Wood, and a barrister of the Inner
Temple. I hope that his new appomtment will not interfere with his
literary work, and look forward to the time when he will give us some
real *“Experiences of a R.M.”

My old friend Hugh Thomson, the famous black-and-white artist,
who 18 on a sketching tour in Galloway for a mew volume of the

)

“ Highways and Byeways’ series, has fallen a victim to the spy

mania and been arrested two or three times, only to be released

when his credemtials were verified. His Ulster accent bewrayeth him,
for the natives know that it is neither English or Scottish, so conclude

_:' ~ that it must be German!

With the single exception of the Central Branch, Sinn Iein, no
public body in Dublin celebrated the Davis centenary! Surely this 1s
not to the credit of Irish letters. ‘What is the National Literary
Society about? If I mistake mot, it celebrated the centenaries of
Mangan, Ferguson and Butt by concerts or lectures, yet it has allowed
the anniversary of the birth of the pure, gifted, high-souled Thomas

Davis to pass without motice. At the meeting above mentioned 1

understand that Mr. Arthur Griffith, an authority on the ‘‘Young
Ireland ”” movement, as evidenced by his fine editions of Mitchel and
Doheny, delivered a glowing eulogium on the life of Davis and his
manifold services to Irelamd.

I present this month an illustration of the statue to Thomas Davis

. from the chisel of Hogan, now standing in the mortuary chapel ab

Mount Jerome. It has had a chequered history. Opigim.ally commis-

NOTICES OF NEW BOOKS. s

Love Sonnkrs. By Padric Gregory. (Belfast: at the Private Pres {
of James M. Harvey.) It s always a pleasure to direct attention f &
the existence of a mew Irish private press, and when that press i l‘:
used for the propagation of pootry of such a high standard as thess
thirty sonnets from the accomplished pen of Mr. Gregory, that plea.sura
is greatly enhanced. In this case one hardly kmows which to prmner
the most; the book or its contents, the printers or the poet. ‘&Ir i -
Gregory is well known to our readers, and the world at large, asa
young poet of great promise—nay, performance—and this collection 0‘1
sonnets, the fruits of his later muse, will undoubtedly add greatly t‘Oi
his fame. An interesting foreword by the author describes the geneosis
of the volume, published at a guinea, and limited to 75 copies. *‘This
book was printed and bound by Edward Wailson, Charles Kerr, Emmet |
Turley, and myself, during the months of August and September m
the year 1914, at the private press of James M. Harvey, 10, Lme;lhall 3
Street, Belfast. The frontispiece ’’—a capital likeness of the author &

—‘“was drawn and lithographed by Seumas Stoupe, at his studio,

College Street, Belfast,”” a happy combination of the various arts. %
é*.‘f

=

e
FRLSTRRE

The hook is a pleasure to handle, a pleasure to read, and leads one
to anticipate eagerly the future output of the poet and the press.
REecoLLECTIONS OF A VIioLiNIsST. By Wm. M. Quirke. (Dawson and ‘{;
Sons.) 3s. 6d. The writer of this most entertaining book, a native of E%
Limerick, has for many years enjoyed a high reputation in musicsl
cgircles in London and Brighfon as a performer on the violin, and in i

soclal circles as a raconteur and humorist of the first water. He

delights in spending his holidays “by the lovely green banks of the |
Suir,” and generally returns laden with a wealth of stories. It has
heen his luck to associate with many people famous in the musical,
literary, theatrical and fashionable worlds, and from all these varied
sourcee he has picked up this book of excellent stories—mot a ‘‘ chest |

nut ”’ amongst them. A keen observer; endowed with true Irish wit, \.

tho possessor of a happy style—he handles the pen as skilfully as the
bow—he has written a volume that no one can help enjoying and long: |
ing for moro, '

like other largo citics, shelters many peculiar types of men and women,
good, bad and indifferent; in fact some, whose knowledge of it 18

SEAMUS O’ CASAIDE.

FORTHCOMING WORKS.

The late Lord O’Brien was engaged for some years prior to his
death in writing his memoirs. These are to be published and the
work will probably be undertaken by his eldest daughter, the Hon.
Georgina O’Brien, a lady of great literary taste and distinction.

Most readers, at any rate most readers who care about literature,
will regard Mr. W. B. Yeats’s ‘‘Memory Harbour: A Revery on My
Childhood and Youth’ as the most promising among the announce-
ments of hooks of an autobiographical character. It will be published
by the Cuala Press, whose fame for good printing and general excel-
lence of book production is due to Mr. Yeats’s sisters.—* The Nation.”’
